title: Diverticulitis
oneLiner: Inflammation of colonic diverticula – usually left-sided – common in >50s; can progress to perforation and generalized peritonitis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Perforated diverticulitis with faecal peritonitis** – single finding: **generalized peritonism** (rigidity, rebound, absent bowel sounds) and/or **septic shock** (tachycardia, hypotension, confusion). Do not be falsely reassured by mild tenderness; if systemic upset is out of proportion, think perforation and call senior.
- **Ischaemic colitis** – mimics diverticulitis but can cause rapid bowel necrosis. Single finding: **bloody diarrhoea** + disproportionate abdominal pain with **raised lactate** >2 mmol/L. Bedside clue: pain is out of proportion to tenderness.
- **Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA)** – uncommon but catastrophic. Single finding: **pulsatile midline mass** + hypotension. Check for AAA in over-60s with back/abdo pain.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  – Left iliac fossa (LIF) pain – constant, may radiate to suprapubic or back  
  – Fever, chills  
  – Anorexia, nausea/vomiting  
  – Altered bowel habit – usually constipation, sometimes diarrhoea  
  – Urinary symptoms (if adjacent to bladder)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  – LIF tenderness ± guarding  
  – Palpable mass (phlegmon/abscess) – deep, tender  
  – ± localised or generalised peritonism (rigidity, rebound)  
  – Bowel sounds reduced or normal initially  
  – Vital signs: tachycardia, pyrexia
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)** – no fever, normal CRP, colicky pain with bowel habit change, relieved by defecation  
- **Renal colic** – pain radiates to groin; no fever (unless infected); haematuria on dipstick  
- **Gynaecological (e.g. ovarian cyst, pelvic inflammatory disease)** – bilateral or suprapubic pain; vaginal discharge; cervical motion tenderness  
- **Ischaemic colitis** – older, atrial fibrillation / embolic risk; bloody diarrhoea; lactate raised  
- **Colorectal carcinoma** – insidious; PR bleeding, weight loss, change in calibre; may present acutely with obstruction  
- **Appendicitis** – if caecum is low or the diverticulum is right-sided (rare)  
- **Splitter:** **Fever + CRP >100 → likely infective (diverticulitis, not IBS)**; **Bloody diarrhoea + lactate >2 → ischaemia, not diverticulitis**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  – Urine dip: exclude UTI (nitrites/leucocytes)  
  – Pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age)  
  – ECG if hypotensive (rule out cardiac cause)  
- **Bloods:**  
  – FBC (WCC raised), CRP (usually >50), U&Es, LFT, lactate, blood cultures if febrile  
  – **Never miss: Lipase?** No – but if epigastric pain is prominent, add lipase to rule out pancreatitis  
- **Imaging:**  
  – **First-line: CT abdomen + IV contrast** – gold standard; shows inflamed diverticulum, phlegmon, abscess, perforation, alternative diagnoses  
  – Second-line: **Transabdominal ultrasound** – if CT contraindicated (pregnancy, contrast allergy); less sensitive for deep abscess  
  – Plain AXR: low yield; only for free air (erect CXR) if perforation suspected  
- **Scoring system:** No widely used risk score; rely on clinical severity + CT findings (modified Hinchey classification: 0=local inflammation, I=phlegmon, II=distant abscess, III=free perforation with purulent peritonitis, IV=faecal peritonitis)  
- **Never-miss test:** **CT abdomen with IV contrast** – omission in a sick patient with LIF pain can miss perforation, abscess, or colon cancer mimicking diverticulitis
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – IV access, 1L Hartmann’s or normal saline bolus if hypotensive  
  – IV antibiotics – broad-spectrum covering gram-negatives & anaerobes (see Drugs)  
  – Analgesia: IV paracetamol 1g; avoid NSAIDs (risk of bleeding/perforation) – add IV morphine titrated if needed (titrate to pain, not to mask peritonism)  
  – NBM if peritonism or vomiting  
  – Surgical review if peritonitis, septic shock, or mass  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  – Continue IV antibiotics  
  – Monitor NEWS2, fluid balance, oral tolerance  
  – If CT shows abscess >3 cm – consider percutaneous drainage (radiology)  
  – Diet: clear fluids → light diet as tolerated (no high-fibre or laxatives)  
  – DVT prophylaxis with LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Complete 5-7 day antibiotic course  
  – Elective colonoscopy after 6 weeks (to exclude underlying malignancy)  
  – Advise high-fibre diet (once acute episode resolved)  
  – Consider elective sigmoid colectomy if: recurrent attacks (≥2), complicated episode (abscess/fistula), immunocompromised patient  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – **Fluids:** IV Hartmann’s 125 mL/h (adjust for renal function)  
  – **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily unless high bleeding risk)  
  – **Diet:** NBM until surgical review clears → then clear fluids  
  – **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (no penicillin allergy):**  
  – Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV 8-hourly (dose adjustment for CrCl <30: 1.2 g IV stat then 600 mg IV 8-hourly) – duration 5-7 days  
  – Or piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV 8-hourly if severe/high-risk (e.g. immunocompromised)  
- **Alternatives (penicillin allergy):**  
  – Ceftriaxone 2 g IV 24-hourly + metronidazole 500 mg IV 8-hourly  
  – Or cefuroxime 750 mg IV 8-hourly + metronidazole 500 mg IV 8-hourly  
- **Duration:** 5-7 days total (IV then switch to oral when improving) – consider oral step-down: co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS or cefalexin 500 mg PO QDS + metronidazole 400 mg PO TDS  
- **Key contraindications:** Penicillin allergy (avoid co-amoxiclav); C. difficile risk with prolonged broad-spectrum abx  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Dose amoxicillin/clavulanate and cephalosporins for CrCl <30; no significant hepatic adjustment  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving antibiotics without checking previous culture results** – leads to inadequate cover for resistant organisms. Always review local antibiogram. Another: **Underdosing in sepsis** – ensure weight-based dosing (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS is standard for most adults; no adjustment needed for obesity unless >120 kg)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  – Perforation with purulent/faecal peritonitis (life-threatening)  
  – Abscess (mesenteric, pelvic) – may require drainage  
  – Sepsis / septic shock  
  – Fistula (colovesical, colovaginal, coloenteric) – pneumaturia, faecaluria, vaginal discharge  
  – Small bowel obstruction due to inflammation  
  – Haemorrhage – can be massive (usually right-sided diverticula)  
- **Chronic:**  
  – Recurrent diverticulitis (20% within 1 year)  
  – Stricture → large bowel obstruction  
  – Colovesical fistula
sections.Prognosis:
- Uncomplicated diverticulitis (Hinchey 0-I): >90% resolution with antibiotics alone.  
- Complicated (Hinchey III-IV): mortality 5-10% with emergency surgery.  
- Recurrence ~20% at 1 year; risk lower after first episode.  
- Most patients who recover without surgery do not have long-term issues.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always request CT with IV contrast** in the acute setting if fever, raised CRP, or atypical presentation – it changes management in up to 30% of cases (e.g., reveals abscess needing drainage).  
- **Start antibiotics immediately** once blood cultures are taken – do not wait for imaging, especially if NEWS2 >3.  
- **Refer for surgical opinion early** if mass, peritonism, >3 cm abscess, or no improvement after 24 h of medical therapy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never give high-fibre diet during the acute attack** – can worsen inflammation. Wait until pain and fever resolved (usually 48-72 h).  
- **Avoid prescribing NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen, diclofenac)** during acute diverticulitis – increase risk of perforation.  
- **Do not dismiss isolated LLQ pain as “just diverticulosis”** – always consider ischaemic colitis, perforated carcinoma, or AAA in at-risk groups.  
- **Never delay urgent surgical review** for peritonitis – faecal peritonitis has a high mortality if not operated within 6-12 hours.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  – Pain worsens or becomes diffuse  
  – Fever (≥38.5°C) develops or persists  
  – Unable to tolerate oral fluids  
  – New vomiting, abdominal distension, or constipation  
  – Blood in stool  
- **Follow-up:**  
  – GP review in 1 week if improving  
  – Outpatient colonoscopy after 6 weeks (to exclude malignancy)  
  – Inform patient of recurrence symptoms and need for early review
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call surgical registrar / on-call immediately if:**  
  – Generalized peritonitis (rigidity, rebound)  
  – NEWS2 ≥5 despite initial fluids  
  – Lactate >2 mmol/L  
  – Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite fluid bolus  
  – GCS <15 or new confusion  
  – Suspected perforation (free air on imaging, or clinical deterioration)  
- **Call ICU/anaesthetic team if:**  
  – Septic shock requiring vasopressors  
  – Respiratory compromise (RR >25, SpO2 <92%)  
  – Need for urgent surgery in haemodynamically unstable patient

rawMarkdown:
## Diverticulitis
**One-liner:** Inflammation of colonic diverticula – usually left-sided – common in >50s; can progress to perforation and generalized peritonitis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Perforated diverticulitis with faecal peritonitis** – single finding: **generalized peritonism** (rigidity, rebound, absent bowel sounds) and/or **septic shock** (tachycardia, hypotension, confusion). Do not be falsely reassured by mild tenderness; if systemic upset is out of proportion, think perforation and call senior.
- **Ischaemic colitis** – mimics diverticulitis but can cause rapid bowel necrosis. Single finding: **bloody diarrhoea** + disproportionate abdominal pain with **raised lactate** >2 mmol/L. Bedside clue: pain is out of proportion to tenderness.
- **Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA)** – uncommon but catastrophic. Single finding: **pulsatile midline mass** + hypotension. Check for AAA in over-60s with back/abdo pain.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  – Left iliac fossa (LIF) pain – constant, may radiate to suprapubic or back  
  – Fever, chills  
  – Anorexia, nausea/vomiting  
  – Altered bowel habit – usually constipation, sometimes diarrhoea  
  – Urinary symptoms (if adjacent to bladder)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  – LIF tenderness ± guarding  
  – Palpable mass (phlegmon/abscess) – deep, tender  
  – ± localised or generalised peritonism (rigidity, rebound)  
  – Bowel sounds reduced or normal initially  
  – Vital signs: tachycardia, pyrexia

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)** – no fever, normal CRP, colicky pain with bowel habit change, relieved by defecation  
- **Renal colic** – pain radiates to groin; no fever (unless infected); haematuria on dipstick  
- **Gynaecological (e.g. ovarian cyst, pelvic inflammatory disease)** – bilateral or suprapubic pain; vaginal discharge; cervical motion tenderness  
- **Ischaemic colitis** – older, atrial fibrillation / embolic risk; bloody diarrhoea; lactate raised  
- **Colorectal carcinoma** – insidious; PR bleeding, weight loss, change in calibre; may present acutely with obstruction  
- **Appendicitis** – if caecum is low or the diverticulum is right-sided (rare)  
- **Splitter:** **Fever + CRP >100 → likely infective (diverticulitis, not IBS)**; **Bloody diarrhoea + lactate >2 → ischaemia, not diverticulitis**

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  – Urine dip: exclude UTI (nitrites/leucocytes)  
  – Pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age)  
  – ECG if hypotensive (rule out cardiac cause)  
- **Bloods:**  
  – FBC (WCC raised), CRP (usually >50), U&Es, LFT, lactate, blood cultures if febrile  
  – **Never miss: Lipase?** No – but if epigastric pain is prominent, add lipase to rule out pancreatitis  
- **Imaging:**  
  – **First-line: CT abdomen + IV contrast** – gold standard; shows inflamed diverticulum, phlegmon, abscess, perforation, alternative diagnoses  
  – Second-line: **Transabdominal ultrasound** – if CT contraindicated (pregnancy, contrast allergy); less sensitive for deep abscess  
  – Plain AXR: low yield; only for free air (erect CXR) if perforation suspected  
- **Scoring system:** No widely used risk score; rely on clinical severity + CT findings (modified Hinchey classification: 0=local inflammation, I=phlegmon, II=distant abscess, III=free perforation with purulent peritonitis, IV=faecal peritonitis)  
- **Never-miss test:** **CT abdomen with IV contrast** – omission in a sick patient with LIF pain can miss perforation, abscess, or colon cancer mimicking diverticulitis

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – IV access, 1L Hartmann’s or normal saline bolus if hypotensive  
  – IV antibiotics – broad-spectrum covering gram-negatives & anaerobes (see Drugs)  
  – Analgesia: IV paracetamol 1g; avoid NSAIDs (risk of bleeding/perforation) – add IV morphine titrated if needed (titrate to pain, not to mask peritonism)  
  – NBM if peritonism or vomiting  
  – Surgical review if peritonitis, septic shock, or mass  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  – Continue IV antibiotics  
  – Monitor NEWS2, fluid balance, oral tolerance  
  – If CT shows abscess >3 cm – consider percutaneous drainage (radiology)  
  – Diet: clear fluids → light diet as tolerated (no high-fibre or laxatives)  
  – DVT prophylaxis with LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Complete 5-7 day antibiotic course  
  – Elective colonoscopy after 6 weeks (to exclude underlying malignancy)  
  – Advise high-fibre diet (once acute episode resolved)  
  – Consider elective sigmoid colectomy if: recurrent attacks (≥2), complicated episode (abscess/fistula), immunocompromised patient  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – **Fluids:** IV Hartmann’s 125 mL/h (adjust for renal function)  
  – **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily unless high bleeding risk)  
  – **Diet:** NBM until surgical review clears → then clear fluids  
  – **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (no penicillin allergy):**  
  – Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV 8-hourly (dose adjustment for CrCl <30: 1.2 g IV stat then 600 mg IV 8-hourly) – duration 5-7 days  
  – Or piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV 8-hourly if severe/high-risk (e.g. immunocompromised)  
- **Alternatives (penicillin allergy):**  
  – Ceftriaxone 2 g IV 24-hourly + metronidazole 500 mg IV 8-hourly  
  – Or cefuroxime 750 mg IV 8-hourly + metronidazole 500 mg IV 8-hourly  
- **Duration:** 5-7 days total (IV then switch to oral when improving) – consider oral step-down: co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS or cefalexin 500 mg PO QDS + metronidazole 400 mg PO TDS  
- **Key contraindications:** Penicillin allergy (avoid co-amoxiclav); C. difficile risk with prolonged broad-spectrum abx  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Dose amoxicillin/clavulanate and cephalosporins for CrCl <30; no significant hepatic adjustment  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving antibiotics without checking previous culture results** – leads to inadequate cover for resistant organisms. Always review local antibiogram. Another: **Underdosing in sepsis** – ensure weight-based dosing (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS is standard for most adults; no adjustment needed for obesity unless >120 kg)

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  – Perforation with purulent/faecal peritonitis (life-threatening)  
  – Abscess (mesenteric, pelvic) – may require drainage  
  – Sepsis / septic shock  
  – Fistula (colovesical, colovaginal, coloenteric) – pneumaturia, faecaluria, vaginal discharge  
  – Small bowel obstruction due to inflammation  
  – Haemorrhage – can be massive (usually right-sided diverticula)  
- **Chronic:**  
  – Recurrent diverticulitis (20% within 1 year)  
  – Stricture → large bowel obstruction  
  – Colovesical fistula

### Prognosis
- Uncomplicated diverticulitis (Hinchey 0-I): >90% resolution with antibiotics alone.  
- Complicated (Hinchey III-IV): mortality 5-10% with emergency surgery.  
- Recurrence ~20% at 1 year; risk lower after first episode.  
- Most patients who recover without surgery do not have long-term issues.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always request CT with IV contrast** in the acute setting if fever, raised CRP, or atypical presentation – it changes management in up to 30% of cases (e.g., reveals abscess needing drainage).  
- **Start antibiotics immediately** once blood cultures are taken – do not wait for imaging, especially if NEWS2 >3.  
- **Refer for surgical opinion early** if mass, peritonism, >3 cm abscess, or no improvement after 24 h of medical therapy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never give high-fibre diet during the acute attack** – can worsen inflammation. Wait until pain and fever resolved (usually 48-72 h).  
- **Avoid prescribing NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen, diclofenac)** during acute diverticulitis – increase risk of perforation.  
- **Do not dismiss isolated LLQ pain as “just diverticulosis”** – always consider ischaemic colitis, perforated carcinoma, or AAA in at-risk groups.  
- **Never delay urgent surgical review** for peritonitis – faecal peritonitis has a high mortality if not operated within 6-12 hours.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  – Pain worsens or becomes diffuse  
  – Fever (≥38.5°C) develops or persists  
  – Unable to tolerate oral fluids  
  – New vomiting, abdominal distension, or constipation  
  – Blood in stool  
- **Follow-up:**  
  – GP review in 1 week if improving  
  – Outpatient colonoscopy after 6 weeks (to exclude malignancy)  
  – Inform patient of recurrence symptoms and need for early review

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call surgical registrar / on-call immediately if:**  
  – Generalized peritonitis (rigidity, rebound)  
  – NEWS2 ≥5 despite initial fluids  
  – Lactate >2 mmol/L  
  – Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite fluid bolus  
  – GCS <15 or new confusion  
  – Suspected perforation (free air on imaging, or clinical deterioration)  
- **Call ICU/anaesthetic team if:**  
  – Septic shock requiring vasopressors  
  – Respiratory compromise (RR >25, SpO2 <92%)  
  – Need for urgent surgery in haemodynamically unstable patient
